test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/regression/tests/REQUEST-931-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RFI/931130.yaml b/tests/regression/tests/REQUEST-931-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RFI/931130.yaml
index d67a67c032..65a7010ccd 100644
--- a/tests/regression/tests/REQUEST-931-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RFI/931130.yaml
+++ b/tests/regression/tests/REQUEST-931-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RFI/931130.yaml
@@ -355,3 +355,19 @@ tests:
         output:
           log:
             expect_ids: [931130]
+  - test_id: 23
+    desc: Remote File Inclusion Attack (931130) with Java backend using file:/
+    stages:
+      - input:
+          dest_addr: 127.0.0.1
+          headers:
+            User-Agent: "OWASP CRS test agent"
+            Host: localhost
+            Accept: text/xml,application/xml,application/xhtml+xml,text/html;q=0.9,text/plain;q=0.8,image/png,*/*;q=0.5
+          method: GET
+          port: 80
+          uri: "/get?x=jar:file:/path/to/myApp.jar!/com/example/data.txt"
+          version: HTTP/1.1
+        output:
+          log:
+            expect_ids: [931130]
problem_statement:
WAF Bypass via jar:file uri schema
Hi team,

While testing the Coraza WAF sandbox, I noticed that the following payload passes without detection or blocking
```bash
curl -ig -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" -H "x-crs-paranoia-level: 3" -H "x-backend: coraza-caddy" --data-urlencode "q=jar:file:/path/to/myApp.jar\!/com/example/data.txt" "https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/"
```
result is
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Wed, 09 Jul 2025 14:28:25 GMT
Content-Type: text/plain
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Connection: keep-alive
X-Unique-ID: aG58idhepdrEuls4eH65lwAAAEg
x-backend: invalid, fallback to apache-latest
x-crs-last-commit: none
```
- The WAF should detect or block this unusual URI scheme input to prevent potential exploitation.


impact of this *If a vulnerable app loads serialized Java objects from a jar:file: URI, it may load attacker-controlled code and execute it. or access to jar archive file*
hints_text:
You are using a bad uri.

This is how it should look like: `jar:file://path/to/myApp.jar\!/com/example/data.txt` (e.g. `file://`

This is catched by rule 931130 at PL2.
> This is how it should look like: `jar:file://path/to/myApp.jar\!/com/example/data.txt` (e.g. `file://`

[RFC8089](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8089#appendix-B) shows that the `file:/path/to` (with a single `/` (slash)) is a valid syntax.

I'm more curious about how can an attacker use this attack? I mean if someone uses a Java application and wants to load a jar file or similar thing (I'm not a Java expert) and he wants to use a user input...? And the user input exists (both the class and data)... How frequent is this?

Btw the original request is blocked on PL4 without any modification:
```
920273 PL4 Invalid character in request (outside of very strict set)
920273 PL4 Invalid character in request (outside of very strict set)
942432 PL4 Restricted SQL Character Anomaly Detection (args): # of special characters exceeded (2)
```

Ok, we should extend the rule to address this new syntax.

[RFC8089](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8089#appendix-B) shows that the `file:/path/to` (with a single `/` (slash)) is a valid syntax.

I'm more curious about how can an attacker use this attack? I mean if someone uses a Java application and wants to load a jar file or similar thing (I'm not a Java expert) and he wants to use a user input...? And the user input exists (both the class and data)... How frequent is this?

Btw the original request is blocked on PL4 without any modification:
```
920273 PL4 Invalid character in request (outside of very strict set)
920273 PL4 Invalid character in request (outside of very strict set)

+++ b/regex-assembly/932340.ra
@@ -0,0 +1,17 @@
+##! Please refer to the documentation at
+##! https://coreruleset.org/docs/development/regex_assembly/.
+
+##! Block Unix commands being executed with no arguments
+##! This rule specifically only blocks commands that can be executed
+##! with no arguments to minimize the risk of false positives.
+
+##!+ i
+
+##!$ $
+
+##!> assemble
+  ##!> include unix-shell-evasion-prefix-start-of-string
+
+  ##! The `@` processor is not defined since we're matching the execution of commands with no arguments.
+  ##!> include-except unix-shell-noarguments unix-shell-noarguments-fps-pl1 -- ~ \S{1,10}\b
+##!<

diff --git a/regex-assembly/toolchain.yaml b/regex-assembly/toolchain.yaml
index 70dc47bd05..c3129af2fe 100644
--- a/regex-assembly/toolchain.yaml
+++ b/regex-assembly/toolchain.yaml
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ patterns:
     # - &|: logical operators in headers, e.g., `a=nc&&$a -nlvp 555`
     # - ): subshell, e.g, `(ifconfig)`
     unix: |
-      (?:[\s<>&|),]|$).*
+      [\s<>&|),].*
     # "more foo", "more,foo", "more;foo", "more.com", "more/e",
     # "more<foo", "more>foo"
     windows: |
@@ -41,11 +41,11 @@ patterns:
     # It will _not_ match:
     # python foo
     #
-    # (?:[<>&|),]|$){1,10}: anti-evasion suffix without space, limited to 10 matches
+    # [<>&|),]{1,10}: anti-evasion suffix without space, limited to 10 matches
     # (?:[\w\d._-][\x5c'\"\[)]*(?:(?:(?:\|\||&&)\s*)?\$[a-z0-9_@?!#{(*-]*)?\x5c?){1,10}: non-whitespace followed by anti-evasion pattern, limited to 10 matches
-    # (?:[\s<>&|),}]|$){1,10}): anti-evasion suffix, including `}` (to match command names within expansions, e.g. `${gcc7.4}gcc7.4`), limited to 10 matches
+    # [\s<>&|),}]{1,10}): anti-evasion suffix, including `}` (to match command names within expansions, e.g. `${gcc7.4}gcc7.4`), limited to 10 matches
     unix: |
-      (?:(?:[<>&|),]|$){1,10}|(?:[\w\d._-][\x5c'\"\[)]*(?:(?:(?:\|\||&&)\s*)?\$[a-z0-9_@?!#{(*-]*)?\x5c?){1,10}(?:[\s<>&|),}]|$){1,10})
+      (?:[<>&|),]{1,10}|(?:[\w\d._-][\x5c'\"\[)]*(?:(?:(?:\|\||&&)\s*)?\$[a-z0-9_@?!#{(*-]*)?\x5c?){1,10}[\s<>&|),}]{1,10})
     # This will match:
     #
     # python,foo
diff --git a/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf b/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf
index 7b2a0d2714..1a1281e404 100644
--- a/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf
+++ b/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf
@@ -94,6 +94,8 @@ SecRule TX:DETECTION_PARANOIA_LEVEL "@lt 1" "id:932012,phase:2,pass,nolog,tag:'O
 #
 #  .932250 (base rule, PL1, targets two and three character commands)
 #  .932260 (base rule, PL1, targets known command word of length > 3 without evasion)
+#  .932340 (PL-1, matches commands with no arguments)
+#  .932350 (PL-3, matches commands with no arguments)

+# [ Unix command injection ]
+#
+# This rule is meant to block the execution of commands with no arguments when it's possible to meaningfully do so.
+#
+# Rule relations:
+#
+#  .932230 (base rule, PL1, targets prefix + two and three character commands)
+#  ..932231 (stricter sibling, PL2, targets prefix + the source shortcut command)
+#  ..932232 (stricter sibling, PL3, targets prefix + additional command words)
+#  .932235 (base rule, PL1, targets prefix + known command word of length > 3 without evasion)
+#
+#  .932250 (base rule, PL1, targets two and three character commands)
+#  .932260 (base rule, PL1, targets known command word of length > 3 without evasion)
+#  .932340 (PL-1, matches commands with no arguments)
+#  .932350 (PL-3, matches commands with no arguments)
+#
+#  .932240 (generic detection, PL2, targets generic evasion attempts)
+#  .932236 (stricter sibling of 932230, 932235, 932250, 932260, PL2,
+#       - with and without prefix
+#       - words of any length)
+#  ..932239 (sibling of 932236, PL2,
+#       - with and without prefix
+#       - words of any length
+#       - targets request headers user-agent and referer only
+#       - excluded words: known user-agents)
+#  ..932238 (stricter sibling of 932236, PL3,
+#       - no excluded words)
+#  .932237 (stricter sibling of 932230, 932235, 932250, 932260, PL3,
+#       - targets request headers user-agent and referer only
+#       - without prefix
+#       - with word boundaries
+#       - words of any length
+#       - excluded words: known user-agents)

problem_statement:
False positive 932250 when sending "sh" parameter
### Description

Keywords "sh" or "zsh" are currently blocked by 932250. I think it's too strict for PL1, in our specific case, this is a search form and users can search for "sh" (beginning of many english words).

### How to reproduce the misbehavior (-> curl call)

```bash
curl -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/\?searchValue\=sh  
932250 PL1 Remote Command Execution: Direct Unix Command Execution
949110 PL1 Inbound Anomaly Score Exceeded (Total Score: 5)
```

### Logs

```
ModSecurity: Warning. RCE bypass via the `date` command
Hello, Everyone

I tried this bypass.

```batch
C:\Users\RelunSec>curl -ig -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" -H "x-crs-paranoia-level: 3" --data-urlencode "q=;date -f /home/test/flag.txt" "https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/"
```

Observed behavior/Response:

```rust
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 22 Dec 2025 07:47:18 GMT
Content-Type: text/plain
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Connection: keep-alive
X-Unique-ID: aUj3hp-Wah1OgdCxchC5KgAAAIE
x-backend: apache-latest
x-crs-last-commit: none
```

Expected behavior/Response:

```rust
HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden
Date: Mon, 22 Dec 2025 07:47:18 GMT
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=iso-8859-1
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Connection: keep-alive
X-Unique-ID: aUj6wt6Zvfw_C_zpED5j9gAAAUI
x-backend: apache-latest
x-crs-last-commit: none

932236 PL2 Remote Command Execution: Unix Command Injection (command without evasion)
949110 PL? Inbound Anomaly Score Exceeded (Total Score: 5)
980170 PL? Anomaly Scores: (Inbound Scores: blocking=5, detection=5, per_pl=0-5-0-0, threshold=5) - (Outbound Scores: blocking=0, detection=0, per_pl=0-0-0-0, threshold=4) - (SQLI=0, XSS=0, RFI=0, LFI=0, RCE=5, PHPI=0, HTTP=0, SESS=0, COMBINED_SCORE=5)
```

Even PL3 the highest level and mostly used in critical services vulnerable to this bypass.

# Impact:
The attacker do not need nothing `date` is already installed in linux and an attacker can read files even other is blocked an attacker can brute force available home files or read files that are not blocked by the WAF, if the .ssh dir blocked an attacker can read /home/testuser/InternalTestReportsCompany.txt without it blocked because it is a custom file.
hints_text: @S0obi Thanks for the report, we'll have to create a new rule that handles blocking the execution of commands with no arguments. This only really needs to be done for a handful of commands.
all_hints_text:
@S0obi Thanks for the report, we'll have to create a new rule that handles blocking the execution of commands with no arguments. This only really needs to be done for a handful of commands.
@S0obi I opened a PR which should resolve similar issues like this in #4273 

False positives like this should be much rarer now since it's now only targeting the handful of commands that can be meaningfully executed with no arguments.

Do you mean the sandbox is failing?
No, the sandbox is fine — the issue is CRS not detecting date -f
I think the bypass here is that the `date` command is trying to read a datefile, fails and then outputs the lines where the failure occured:
```
$ date -f test.txt
date: invalid date ‘Hello’
date: invalid date ‘World!’
date: invalid date ‘db password is: password’
```

This will probably need it's own dedicated rule to reduce false positives.
Hello, thanks for the feedback.
I agree that the date -f behavior looks like it is just parsing a datefile and failing on invalid lines. However, from an attacker’s perspective this is still command execution with file read capability:

The -f option makes date read arbitrary files line‑by‑line.

Even if the lines are invalid dates, the command still leaks file contents back to the client in error messages.

This means an attacker can exfiltrate sensitive data from custom files that are not otherwise blocked by the WAF.

Since date is installed by default on most Linux distributions, this vector is broadly exploitable without extra tooling.

So while it may look like a false positive case, in practice it is a file disclosure primitive via command injection. That’s why I expected PL3 to block it -- the risk is equivalent to other RCE payloads.

I recommend adding a dedicated detection rule for date -f usage (and possibly other date options that read from files), so that this bypass is covered without introducing noise for normal date usage.

patch:
diff --git a/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf b/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf
index c33035b7ff..ade6f51134 100644
--- a/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf
+++ b/rules/REQUEST-932-APPLICATION-ATTACK-RCE.conf
@@ -756,6 +756,10 @@ SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|XML:/* "@rx \ba[\"
 # that affect the behavior of the web server, possibly causing remote
 # code execution.
 #
+# The inverted chained rule enforces a word boundary for some entries that are prone to
+# being detected as false positives. This can't be enforced for all entries since some are intentionally meant to match permutations.
+#
+#
 SecRule FILES|REQUEST_HEADERS:X-Filename|REQUEST_HEADERS:X_Filename|REQUEST_HEADERS:X-File-Name "@pmFromFile restricted-upload.data" \
     "id:932180,\
     phase:2,\
@@ -774,8 +778,11 @@ SecRule FILES|REQUEST_HEADERS:X-Filename|REQUEST_HEADERS:X_Filename|REQUEST_HEAD
     tag:'capec/1000/152/248/88',\
     ver:'OWASP_CRS/4.21.0-dev',\
     severity:'CRITICAL',\
-    setvar:'tx.rce_score=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}',\
-    setvar:'tx.inbound_anomaly_score_pl1=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}'"
+    chain"
+    SecRule MATCHED_VARS "!@rx (?i)(?:\.boto|buddyinfo|mtrr|acpi|zoneinfo)\B" \
+        "t:none,\
+        setvar:'tx.rce_score=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}',\
+        setvar:'tx.inbound_anomaly_score_pl1=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}'"
 
 
 # [ Windows command injection ]

problem_statement:
Rule 932180 FP on Filename Substring
### Description

Rule 932180 triggers for substrings in filenames. See `curl` examples below.

### How to reproduce the misbehavior (-> curl call)

This example is likely a user's initials (JAC).
```sh
echo "contents" | curl -X POST -F "file1=@-;filename=JACpic.jpg;type=text/plain" -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/
```

```
932180 PL1 Restricted File Upload Attempt
949110 PL? Inbound Anomaly Score Exceeded (Total Score: 5)
980170 PL? Anomaly Scores: (Inbound Scores: blocking=5, detection=5, per_pl=5-0-0-0, threshold=5) - (Outbound Scores: blocking=0, detection=0, per_pl=0-0-0-0, threshold=4) - (SQLI=0, XSS=0, RFI=0, LFI=0, RCE=5, PHPI=0, HTTP=0, SESS=0, COMBINED_SCORE=5)
```

This one triggers  due to `boto`.
```sh
echo "contents" | curl -X POST -F "file1=@-;filename=abc.botox.jpg;type=text/plain" -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/
```

```
932180 PL1 Restricted File Upload Attempt
949110 PL? Inbound Anomaly Score Exceeded (Total Score: 5)
980170 PL? Anomaly Scores: (Inbound Scores: blocking=5, detection=5, per_pl=5-0-0-0, threshold=5) - (Outbound Scores: blocking=0, detection=0, per_pl=0-0-0-0, threshold=4) - (SQLI=0, XSS=0, RFI=0, LFI=0, RCE=5, PHPI=0, HTTP=0, SESS=0, COMBINED_SCORE=5)
```

This triggers due to `mtrr`
```sh
echo "contents" | curl -X POST -F "file1=@-;filename=amtrra.jpg;type=text/plain" -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/
```

```
932180 PL1 Restricted File Upload Attempt
949110 PL? Inbound Anomaly Score Exceeded (Total Score: 5)
980170 PL? Anomaly Scores: (Inbound Scores: blocking=5, detection=5, per_pl=5-0-0-0, threshold=5) - (Outbound Scores: blocking=0, detection=0, per_pl=0-0-0-0, threshold=4) - (SQLI=0, XSS=0, RFI=0, LFI=0, RCE=5, PHPI=0, HTTP=0, SESS=0, COMBINED_SCORE=5)
```

### Your Environment

* CRS version (e.g., v3.3.4): 4.18.0
* Paranoia level setting (e.g. PL1) : PL1
* ModSecurity version (e.g., 2.9.6): 3.0.14
* Web Server and version or cloud provider / CDN (e.g., Apache httpd 2.4.54): Nginx 1.26
* Operating System and version: Amazon Linux 2

### Confirmation

[X] I have removed any personal data (email addresses, IP addresses,
    passwords, domain names) from any logs posted.

diff --git a/regex-assembly/933160.ra b/regex-assembly/933160.ra
index e63c612e4b..9c8ab4d7ab 100644
--- a/regex-assembly/933160.ra
+++ b/regex-assembly/933160.ra
@@ -41,6 +41,7 @@ md5
 mkdir
 opendir
 passthru
+phpinfo
 popen
 printf
 readfile
@@ -53,9 +54,11 @@ usort
 ##! English words, or potential snippets of them, are added here to perform a regex match.
 ##! Compared to the parallel match performed by 933150, fewer false positives will be generated.
 exp
+intval
 ord
 prev
 stat
 substr
 system
 unlink
+unserialize
diff --git a/rules/REQUEST-933-APPLICATION-ATTACK-PHP.conf b/rules/REQUEST-933-APPLICATION-ATTACK-PHP.conf
index 639169fb27..39c3af59db 100644
--- a/rules/REQUEST-933-APPLICATION-ATTACK-PHP.conf
+++ b/rules/REQUEST-933-APPLICATION-ATTACK-PHP.conf
@@ -279,13 +279,13 @@ SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|XML:/* "@rx (?:bzi
 # The list of PHP functions is divided into four groups of varying attack/false positive risk.
 # Four separate rules are used to detect these groups of functions:
 #
-# - Rule 933150: ~237 words highly common to PHP injection payloads and extremely rare in
+# - Rule 933150: ~234 words highly common to PHP injection payloads and extremely rare in
 #		natural language or other contexts.
 #		Examples: 'base64_decode', 'file_get_contents'.
 #		These words are detected as a match directly using @pmFromFile.
 #		Function names are defined in php-function-names-933150.data
 #
-# - Rule 933160: ~36 words which are common in PHP code, but have a higher chance to cause
+# - Rule 933160: ~39 words which are common in PHP code, but have a higher chance to cause
 #		false positives in natural language or other contexts.
 #		Examples: 'chr', 'eval'.
 #		To mitigate false positives, a regexp looks for PHP function syntax, e.g. 'eval()'.
@@ -312,8 +312,10 @@ SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|XML:/* "@rx (?:bzi
 #
 # Rule 933150 contains a small list of function names which are highly indicative of a PHP
 # injection attack, for example 'base64_decode'.
-# We block these function names outright, without using a complex regexp or chain.
-# This could make the detection a bit more robust against possible bypasses.
+# The rule uses a chained rule to require parentheses after function names.
+# This prevents false positives from substring matches (e.g., "intval" in "PaintValdosta").
+# Functions that commonly appear as English words/substrings are handled by rule 933160
+# with stricter regex-based function call syntax validation.
 #
 SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|REQUEST_FILENAME|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|XML:/* "@pmFromFile php-function-names-933150.data" \
     "id:933150,\
@@ -333,8 +335,11 @@ SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|REQUEST_FILENAME|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|X
     tag:'capec/1000/152/242',\
     ver:'OWASP_CRS/4.23.0-dev',\
     severity:'CRITICAL',\
-    setvar:'tx.php_injection_score=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}',\
-    setvar:'tx.inbound_anomaly_score_pl1=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}'"
+    chain"
+    SecRule MATCHED_VARS "@pm ( )" \
+        "t:none,\
+        setvar:'tx.php_injection_score=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}',\
+        setvar:'tx.inbound_anomaly_score_pl1=+%{tx.critical_anomaly_score}'"
 
 
 #
@@ -368,7 +373,7 @@ SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|REQUEST_FILENAME|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|X
 # (consult https://coreruleset.org/docs/development/regex_assembly/ for details):
 #   crs-toolchain regex update 933160
 #
-SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|REQUEST_FILENAME|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|XML:/* "@rx (?i)\b\(?[\"']*(?:assert(?:_options)?|c(?:hr|reate_function)|e(?:val|x(?:ec|p))|f(?:ile(?:group)?|open|puts)|glob|i(?:mage(?:gif|(?:jpe|pn)g|wbmp|xbm)|s_a)|m(?:d5|kdir)|o(?:pendir|rd)|p(?:assthru|open|r(?:intf|ev))|r(?:eadfile|trim)|s(?:t(?:rip_tags|at)|ubstr|ystem)|tmpfile|u(?:n(?:pac|lin)k|sort))(?:/(?:\*.*?\*/|/[^\n\r]*)|#[^\n\r]*|[\s\x0b\"])*[\"']*\)?[\s\x0b]*\([^\)]*\)" \
+SecRule REQUEST_COOKIES|REQUEST_COOKIES_NAMES|REQUEST_FILENAME|ARGS_NAMES|ARGS|XML:/* "@rx (?i)\b\(?[\"']*(?:assert(?:_options)?|c(?:hr|reate_function)|e(?:val|x(?:ec|p))|f(?:ile(?:group)?|open|puts)|glob|i(?:mage(?:gif|(?:jpe|pn)g|wbmp|xbm)|s_a|ntval)|m(?:d5|kdir)|o(?:pendir|rd)|p(?:assthru|hpinfo|open|r(?:intf|ev))|r(?:eadfile|trim)|s(?:t(?:rip_tags|at)|ubstr|ystem)|tmpfile|u(?:n(?:(?:pac|lin)k|serialize)|sort))(?:/(?:\*.*?\*/|/[^\n\r]*)|#[^\n\r]*|[\s\x0b\"])*[\"']*\)?[\s\x0b]*\([^\)]*\)" \
     "id:933160,\
     phase:2,\
     block,\
diff --git a/rules/php-function-names-933150.data b/rules/php-function-names-933150.data
index adc6afc2b0..c4b2573f6a 100644
--- a/rules/php-function-names-933150.data
+++ b/rules/php-function-names-933150.data
@@ -98,7 +98,6 @@ imagegd2
 ini_get
 ini_get_all
 ini_set
-intval
 iptcembed
 is_array
 is_dir
@@ -149,7 +148,6 @@ pg_prepare
 pg_query
 php_strip_whitespace
 php_uname
-phpinfo
 phpversion
 posix_getegid
 posix_geteuid
@@ -231,7 +229,6 @@ strtoupper

-unserialize

problem_statement:
Rule 933150 Has False Positive for PHP Functions in Substrings
### Description

From https://github.com/coreruleset/coreruleset/issues/3641#issuecomment-3525344886.

Rule 933150 flags substrings for PHP functions. For example, `intval` in "PaintValdosta". This is particularly an issue with URLs where multiple words are concatenated.

### How to reproduce the misbehavior (-> curl call)

```sh
curl -H "x-format-output: txt-matched-rules" https://sandbox.coreruleset.org/PaintValdosta
```

```
933150 PL1 PHP Injection Attack: High-Risk PHP Function Name Found
949110 PL? Inbound Anomaly Score Exceeded (Total Score: 5)
980170 PL? Anomaly Scores: (Inbound Scores: blocking=5, detection=5, per_pl=5-0-0-0, threshold=5) - (Outbound Scores: blocking=0, detection=0, per_pl=0-0-0-0, threshold=4) - (SQLI=0, XSS=0, RFI=0, LFI=0, RCE=0, PHPI=5, HTTP=0, SESS=0, COMBINED_SCORE=5)
```

### Confirmation

[X] I have removed any personal data (email addresses, IP addresses,
    passwords, domain names) from any logs posted.
all_hints_text:
@EsadCetiner That’s the issue I’m raising regarding the redistribution of datasets based on false-positive risk.

This particular set is one of those I plan to split. Sequences shorter than 5 characters should be isolated, as well as those shorter than 7 characters that match `[\w\/]+.`

If you want another FP : saintvalentin.jpg
@ssigwart I've opened a PR at #4340 which should resolve this issue.
> If you want another FP : saintvalentin.jpg

Good one.

> [@ssigwart](https://github.com/ssigwart) I've opened a PR at [#4340](https://github.com/coreruleset/coreruleset/pull/4340) which should resolve this issue.

Thanks, @EsadCetiner. Looking at https://github.com/coreruleset/coreruleset/blob/main/rules/php-function-names-933150.data, here are some other false positives that I can think of:

- `fclose` -> "SelfCloseDoors"
- `fwrite` -> I feel like there could be something here.
- `getenv` -> "GetEnvironmentalImpact"

They might be a bit contrived, but I wonder if rule 933150 could be adjusted a little instead of moving possible FPs to rule 933160. If seems like rule 933150 should require a boundary character on either side. I see it uses `@pmFromFile` and I don't know much about that, but maybe there's some other function that searches for boundaries.
@ssigwart 
> here are some other false positives that I can think of:

I see where your coming from and I see the same issue as well, are these actual false positives you encountered? The only reason why I haven't made a bigger change to these rules as it seems like these false positives are relatively small and just edge cases.

> should require a boundary character on either side

Yes that's the main issue here

> I see it uses @pmFromFile and I don't know much about that

Unfortunately the `@pmFromFile` operator isn't very flexible, it's pretty much just a simple list. The rule is likely using that operator for performance reasons, iirc there was an attempt to benchmark the performance difference between `@rx` and `@pmFromFile` a while back but nothing has come of it so far. I'm trying to be cautious in terms of performance.
"PaintValdosta" is a real one. The others were just me trying to think of somewhat plausible ones for general use cases. They probably wouldn't impact my site. i did didn't want you to be adding rules specifically for my use cases since I could create custom rules to exclude them.

I wonder if there's some way to use `@pmFromFile` to do the initial match, then chain it to use some other operative like `@rx` to see if it's a boundary. Would it be a performance issue for the rule to have a large `@rx` if it rarely was executed?
@ssigwart 

> I wonder if there's some way to use @pmFromFile to do the initial match, then chain it to use some other operative like @rx to see if it's a boundary. Would it be a performance issue for the rule to have a large @rx if it rarely was executed?

I actually had that idea a few minutes after I posted my comment, I'm taking a look at it now. Checking for a few special characters should be enough. And yes, there shouldn't be any performance issues since the first chained rule will need to match first before the second one runs.
@ssigwart I've updated my PR to add a chained rule
Thanks, @EsadCetiner. That definitely seems like an improvement to me. I forget exactly what `MATCHED_VARS` returns, but would something like `https://example.com/SelfCloseDoors#details` still be an issue because it has `#`? Actually, would the dots trigger it too?

Regardless, I think it's worth merging your PR. I'm getting into smaller and smaller edge cases.
> would something like https://example.com/SelfCloseDoors#details still be an issue because it has `#`

Yes, since 933160 is checking for those characters (It's also checking for slashes) I added those characters to the chained rule. I don't have much experience with writing PHP code, but looking at 933160 it looks like the parenthesis `()` is the really important part here, I could be wrong so I'm just being cautious. 
I write PHP all the time. I don't think any characters besides `(` and `)` are needed since functions are executed with `()`. One thing that we probably need someone else's feedback on is whether `/get?base64_deCOde` really should be blocked. I think a more realistic URL would be `/get?action=base64_deCOde`. Potentially, if someone used `eval($_GET['action'])` or `$_GET['action']()`, it would be exploitable even though there's no parenthesis. That would be terribly insecure code though, so I don't know if that's what this rule is trying to block or something else.

I also took a look at real attack attempts on the site I manage. Here are a few examples:

```
GET /?s=../%5Cthink%5CContainer/invokefunction&function=call_user_func_array&vars%5B0%5D=md5&vars%5B1%5D%5B%5D=HelloThinkPHP
```

I found https://www.akamai.com/blog/security/thinkphp-exploit-actively-exploited-in-the-wild. So it looks like some PHP library had a vulnerability that didn't involve parenthesis.

The other thing that I see a lot of are requests like these:
```
GET /phpinfo
GET /phpinfo.php
```

`phpinfo` is a function in PHP to show system information. I think this is a special case though. People often would name a page the same as the function. I think that `phpinfo` could deserve it's own rule for checking the filename so that you don't need to consider it too much in this rule because it's a different animal.
> I write PHP all the time. I don't think any characters besides ( and ) are needed since functions are executed with (). 

Thanks for confirming my suspicion, after digging a bit further I think that's all that needs to be checked in the chained rule. I think 933160 is checking for characters such as `#` to take into account evasions, that shouldn't an issue with 933150's chained rule.

> One thing that we probably need someone else's feedback on is whether /get?base64_deCOde really should be blocked

Sorry, I don't follow, I'm not sure how using the md5 transformation here would be useful, although this issue isn't specific to 933150.
That makes more sense. It's not that it didn't detect them before. It's that that wasn't the intent. Sounds good.

For the MD5 transformations, my understanding is that, in general, `@pmFromFile` is preferred because it is fast, but the downside is that it is a substring match. There are places where it would be better for a rule to match full words. So I was thinking that if `@pmFromFile` was used for a quick test to see if there's a substring, a chain could be use to do a more thorough check for the full work. If the chain could split the match into words and then do an MD5 on the words (maybe lowercase words), the comparison of the MD5 to an MD5 list with `@pmFromFile` would be fast and eliminate false positives.

For example, with the `PaintValdosta` example, it would compare the MD5 of `paintvaldosta` (`4feae1dc3fa8cb1b20788c8b5709e23f`) with that of `intval` (`fb42920bc738a35518e3109fc5b51dc6`) and since they don't match, it would know that it wasn't a full substring match.

Another option would be to add a chain after `@pmFromFile` to do an `@rx` of the same function list, but with boundary conditions similar to this:
https://github.com/coreruleset/coreruleset/blob/e57b677877822aa9ce5d1b02f25ef644a5e2637d/rules/REQUEST-933-APPLICATION-ATTACK-PHP.conf#L373

However, I don't know if having a huge `@rx` in the rules config file would have a performance impact. If not, that might be the way to go for rules that want boundary conditions, but wants to do a quick preliminary check with `@pmFromFile`.
> my understanding is that, in general, @pmFromFile is preferred because it is fast, but the downside is that it is a substring match

Yes that's correct.

> If the chain could split the match into words and then do an MD5 on the words (maybe lowercase words), the comparison of the MD5 to an MD5 list with @pmFromFile would be fast and eliminate false positives.

The MD5 transformation will transform the entire matched parameter, you'd need to use a regex backreference to extract the specific function without any substring matches then add the extra checks. Although backreferences are [not allowed due to ReDoS risk and compatibility with non-backtracking regex engines](https://coreruleset.org/docs/6-development/6-1-contribution-guidelines/#writing-regular-expressions-for-non-backtracking-compatibility) (I see your thought process, but I think you misunderstand how transformations work). 933150 doesn't include all PHP functions, so naked functions like `fputs` or `fopen` will still be affected by said issue even if this worked as we expected hypothetically.

> However, I don't know if having a huge @rx in the rules config file would have a performance impact. If not, that might be the way to go for rules that want boundary conditions, but wants to do a quick preliminary check with @pmFromFile.

I think we'd have to move all the rules to a regex anyways, I don't see a way to do it with the `@pmFromFile` operator at all even with chained rules. It's better if you open another issue to discuss this, and we can handle the false positive issue for this rule for now. This discussion is starting to get a bit off topic anyways.
